headline: Wage Subsidy Programme Cost RM18B But Saved Only 62% of Targeted Jobs
context: Post-pandemic audit of the Wage Subsidy Programme found it cost RM18 billion from 2020-2023 but preserved only 62% of the 2.8 million jobs it targeted, with RM4.2 billion going to companies that would have retained workers anyway.

cards.big: RM18 billion spent on wage subsidies — but RM4.2 billion went to companies that didn't actually need the help
cards.sub: Deadweight loss of 23% means almost a quarter of funds supported jobs that were never at risk

cards.big: Cost per job saved averaged RM10,400 — but varied from RM3,200 for genuine SMEs to RM28,000 for large listed companies
cards.sub: 42 Bursa-listed firms received RM1.8 billion in wage subsidies while paying RM3.2 billion in dividends in same period

cards.big: 680,000 workers lost jobs despite employer receiving wage subsidies — companies took funds then retrenched anyway
cards.sub: Workers in retail, hospitality, and aviation were most affected — sectors where subsidy reached companies too late

cards.big: Auditor General found 14,200 duplicate claims and 8,400 claims from non-existent businesses totalling RM380 million in fraud
cards.sub: Rapid disbursement without verification enabled systematic abuse — prosecution rate for fraudulent claims was under 2%

cards.big: Emergency spending is understandable — but RM18 billion without outcome tracking or fraud prevention is taxpayer money burned blind
Should future emergency spending programmes require real-time beneficiary verification and mandatory post-disbursement audits?
